original_hypothesis.title: unlockOverride Enables Infinite Voting Power Cycling Attack
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When unlockOverride is enabled, users can withdraw locked CANTO early. However, GaugeController is NOT notified of the withdrawal — the user's gauge votes remain active until the original 5-year lock expiration. An attacker can: lock CANTO → vote for gauge → withdraw early → transfer CANTO to new address → lock again → vote again, multiplying gauge weight with a fixed capital amount.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: VotingEscrow.withdraw zeroes the lock and checkpoints VE state, but GaugeController's vote_user_slopes, vote_user_power, and gauge weight points remain unchanged. The stale votes persist and decay based on the original lock_end, not the actual withdrawal. No cross-contract notification exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() zeroes locked[msg.sender] and checkpoints VE, but no call to GaugeController to invalidate votes; GaugeController still has active vote_user_slopes for the user

preconditions: Attacker can create multiple addresses (or use one address cyclically)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: After withdraw: locked[user].amount=0 but vote_user_slopes[user][gauge] still active with original slope and end time
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Each cycle adds the full veCANTO slope to gauge weight; N cycles = N× gauge weight from fixed capital

economic_questions: How frequently has unlockOverride been toggled historically?
What is the maximum gauge weight inflation achievable per cycle?
Can the attacker profit by directing inflated emissions to their own market position?
validation_requirements: Confirm GaugeController votes persist after VE withdrawal
Test lock→vote→withdraw→relock→vote cycle and measure gauge weight
Calculate emissions captured per cycle vs capital cost

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Any amount of CANTO (recycled across cycles)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for lock+vote+withdraw per cycle (~0.05 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Linear in number of cycles: N cycles = N× normal gauge influence
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Unbounded multiplier on effective voting power
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: As many cycles as gas budget allows

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Fixed amount of CANTO, reused across cycles
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete capture of gauge emissions by attacker-favored markets

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional dilution of their gauge influence
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All CANTO emissions for affected epochs

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate multi-cycle attack and measure gauge weight inflation

validation_result.mechanism: Cross-contract stale vote persistence after early withdrawal
validation_result.impact_summary: When unlockOverride is enabled, withdrawing from VotingEscrow does not invalidate GaugeController votes; attacker can cycle CANTO across addresses to multiply gauge weight with fixed capital.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a critical cross-contract state synchronization defect. VotingEscrow.withdraw() zeroes the user's lock (amount, end, delegated) and checkpoints the VE state to reflect zero voting power, but it makes no call to GaugeController to invalidate the user's gauge votes. GaugeController stores vote_user_slopes[user][gauge], vote_user_power[user], and has contributed to points_weight[gauge][time], changes_weight[gauge][lock_end], points_sum[type][time], and changes_sum[type][lock_end]. All of these persist after withdrawal. An attacker using multiple addresses can lock the same CANTO, vote, withdraw early, transfer to a new address, and repeat - each cycle creates a new stale vote contribution to gauge weight that persists until the original lock_end (~5 years). VotingEscrow has no reference to GaugeController (checked constructor and state variables), so it structurally cannot notify it.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: VotingEscrow.withdraw() at lines 248-269 clearly shows: (1) lock is zeroed, (2) _checkpoint is called to update VE state, (3) CANTO is sent back, (4) NO interaction with GaugeController. GaugeController.vote_for_gauge_weights() at line ~330 stores persistent vote data in vote_user_slopes and contributes to points_weight. The _get_weight() function at lines ~171-192 fills in gauge weight history based on stored slopes and changes_weight, which include the stale votes. There is no mechanism in any function of GaugeController that checks current VE balance before applying stored votes.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: After zeroing the lock and checkpointing VE state, no call is made to GaugeController to invalidate the user's gauge votes. The user's vote_user_slopes, contributions to points_weight, and scheduled changes_weight entries all persist, maintaining stale gauge weight.

Stores persistent vote data (slope, power, end) keyed by user address. These values are never invalidated when the user withdraws from VotingEscrow. The stored end time references the original lock_end, causing votes to persist for the full lock duration even after withdrawal.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls VotingEscrow.createLock(value) from Address A - locks CANTO for 5 years

2. Attacker calls GaugeController.vote_for_gauge_weights(gauge, 10000) from Address A - allocates 100% power to gauge, stores vote_user_slopes[A][gauge] with slope and lock_end ~5 years out, updates points_weight[gauge] and changes_weight[gauge][lock_end]
4. Attacker calls VotingEscrow.withdraw() from Address A - locked[A] zeroed, VE checkpoint records zero power, CANTO returned. NO call to GaugeController.

5. GaugeController state unchanged: vote_user_slopes[A][gauge] still has active slope/end, points_weight[gauge] still includes A's contribution, changes_weight[gauge][lock_end_A] still schedules slope removal in ~5 years
6. Attacker transfers CANTO to Address B
7. Address B calls VotingEscrow.createLock(value) - creates new lock with new 5-year end
8. Address B calls GaugeController.vote_for_gauge_weights(gauge, 10000) - adds SECOND vote contribution to same gauge
9. Gauge now has contributions from BOTH Address A (stale) AND Address B (new) - double the weight from same CANTO
10. Repeat for N addresses to get N× gauge weight
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The core defect is at VotingEscrow.sol:248-269 where withdraw() completes without any cross-contract notification. VotingEscrow does not hold a reference to GaugeController (checked constructor at line 85-92 and all state variables at lines 28-44) and thus structurally cannot notify it. GaugeController._get_weight() (lines 171-192) fills gauge weight history by iterating through stored points_weight and changes_weight, which still contain the stale vote contributions. GaugeController._gauge_relative_weight() (lines 226-236) reads points_weight[_gauge][t].bias which includes stale contributions, inflating the gauge's relative weight used by LendingLedger for reward distribution.

deployment_assumptions_checked: unlockOverride is a governance-togglable boolean, not a deployment config
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.sol shows standard deployment without setting unlockOverride. The unlockOverride defaults to false and must be toggled by governance via toggleUnlockOverride(). This is a runtime governance decision, not a deployment assumption.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid. Step-by-step: (1) Address A locks CANTO → createLock succeeds with locked_.amount == 0 check. (2) Address A votes → vote_for_gauge_weights stores slope and updates gauge weight. (3) unlockOverride must be enabled by governance. (4) Address A withdraws → withdraw() passes with unlockOverride == true, zeroes VE state, returns CANTO. (5) GaugeController retains all vote data - verified by examining withdraw() which has no external calls except the CANTO transfer. (6) CANTO transferred to Address B via native transfer. (7) Address B creates lock → createLock succeeds (new address, no prior lock). (8) Address B votes for same gauge → vote_for_gauge_weights creates new entry in vote_user_slopes[B][gauge], adds NEW slope to points_weight[gauge]. (9) Gauge weight now includes both A's stale and B's new contributions. The only precondition that requires external action is unlockOverride being enabled, which is a legitimate governance function.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. When unlockOverride is active, an attacker can multiply gauge weight linearly with the number of addresses used, all from a fixed amount of CANTO. This directly controls CANTO emission distribution via LendingLedger, which uses gauge_relative_weight_write() to determine per-market rewards. The attacker can capture a disproportionate share of all CANTO emissions for as long as the stale votes persist (up to 5 years per cycle). All legitimate veCANTO voters suffer proportional dilution of their gauge influence.
recommended_mitigations: Add a reference to GaugeController in VotingEscrow and call a notification function during withdraw() to invalidate the user's gauge votes
In GaugeController.vote_for_gauge_weights(), verify the user's current VE balance/slope at the time of gauge weight computation (not just at vote time)
Implement a vote invalidation mechanism in GaugeController that checks VE lock status before applying stored votes during _get_weight() or _get_sum()
When unlockOverride is enabled, require users to first remove their gauge votes (vote 0 on all gauges) before allowing withdrawal
Consider removing unlockOverride entirely and implementing a penalty-based early exit (quitLock) that also clears gauge votes
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VotingEscrow, GaugeController, LendingLedger. 2. Add a gauge type and gauge. 3. From Address A, createLock with 100 CANTO. 4. From Address A, vote_for_gauge_weights(gauge, 10000). 5. Record gauge weight via get_gauge_weight(gauge). 6. Toggle unlockOverride. 7. From Address A, withdraw(). 8. Verify gauge weight unchanged via get_gauge_weight(gauge) and checkpoint_gauge(gauge). 9. Transfer CANTO to Address B. 10. From Address B, createLock with same 100 CANTO. 11. From Address B, vote_for_gauge_weights(gauge, 10000). 12. Advance time by 1 week, checkpoint gauge. 13. Verify gauge weight is approximately 2x what a single 100 CANTO lock would produce. 14. Repeat for Address C, D to show linear scaling.
uncertainties: The practical impact depends on how frequently governance enables unlockOverride and for how long it remains active
Gas costs on Canto network may affect the practical number of cycles an attacker would execute
Governance could theoretically remove the gauge or use remove_gauge_weight to mitigate (though remove_gauge is itself broken per INV-STATE-001)
additional_testing_needed: Quantify exact gauge weight inflation per cycle in a fork test
Test whether the attack works with same-address cycling (lock→vote→withdraw→relock→vote on different gauge)
Measure the impact on LendingLedger reward distribution with inflated gauge weights
Verify that vote_user_power tracking on the original address prevents same-address same-gauge re-voting without clearing old votes first
